pages_preview.text_preview: Preregistration Review Manual 5/5/21 All SCORE projects are pre-registered prior to data collection or analysis (for projects not collecting new human data). This document details the procedure of pre-registration review for both Reviewers and Editors. An important part of the SCORE project is this pre-registration review period. It allows the investigator to receive feedback on the details of their replication, reproduction, or generalizability project, and gives the original authors of the focal paper an opportunity to weigh in (or comment retrospectively) on the methodology of the project attempt. The following sections detail the process of pre-registration review. The Appendix contains a directory of Editor and Reviewer assignments for ease of recruitment. 1. Pre-registration draft received by COS and assigned to an Editor COS research scientists work with “RR labs” (labs working on a replication, reproduction, or generalizability project) to get them set up to write a pre-registration of their project. Once they are satisfied with the document, COS will source that document to the field-relevant editor. COS will send the following email to the Editor when a pre-registration document is ready for the review process: ------ Subject: SCORE Preregistration ready for review: Original Author Last Name (year) Journal Hello, A new pre-registration is available for immediate review. Please begin to recruit reviewers to begin the review process for this project. Link to Pre-Registration: [LINK TO PREREG WITH COMMENT PERMISSIONS] As a reminder, the goal timeline for each review is as follows: ● As soon as possible, but no later than 2 days from when the prereg is sourced to an editor: Reviewers are identified and signed onto the cover page of the pre-registration ready to review. ● Within 1 - 2 days, but no later than 5 days from the start of the review period: Reviewers begin to comment on the pre-registration, complete their review, and notify the Editor that their review is complete. ● Within 7 days from the start of the review period: All comments have been addressed by RR lab and Reviewers. Editor notifies COS coordination team with a final

determination as to whether the protocol described is a sufficiently high fidelity, good faith project to continue. The main contact for the RR lab is cc’d to this email [CONTACT INFO]. If you need to directly reach out to them via email (as opposed to commenting to them in the pre-registration document), please use that address. Please let us know your final decision on the acceptability of this protocol once 1. The RR lab has notified you that they are finished addressing comments, and 2. You have completed your final review of this protocol [NEW for Dec 2021] Please use the pre-reg check in form, linked here, to finalize the review and grant your final approval of the review process: [ASANA LINK HERE] For easy access, you can access the pre-registration review manual by clicking here. Thank you so much for your work on this! Cheers, Nick, Olivia, and Krystal ----- The specific email that you receive will have the unique link for the pre-registration ready to review, as well as the contact information of the lead author of the pre-registration. Once the pre-registration is sent to the Editor, the COS team will mark this date, and will follow the process to ensure timely completion of the review. At 7 days, the COS team will reach out to the Editor if they have not heard back or have concerns about the progression of the review. 2. Sourcing Reviewers Once you have received the above email from COS, please reach out to your Reviewer pool to let them know of the opportunity (CCing scorecoordinator@cos.io). We recommend reaching out to the entire list at once, and then choosing two Reviewers who are available and respond quickly. Remember, every review is worth $125 for each Reviewer, so please try and rotate through Reviewers (when possible) to maximize opportunity, instead of having 2-3 “go-to” favorite Reviewers. This also ensures review diversity across our pre-registrations, which is useful for our projects on the whole. Editors should use their best judgment to ensure a “good faith, high quality” review process.

Below is an example email Editors could send to source Reviewers, but we encourage Editors to make it their own as they will direct this part of the process. The main goals are to show Reviewers the pre-registration (to see if they are interested) and to remind Reviewers of the timeline to try and source those who are available for the upcoming 7 day review window. ----- Subject: SCORE Preregistration Review Opportunity: Original Paper Title (year) Dear SCORE Reviewers, A new pre-registration is available for immediate review. Please briefly review the draft to gauge your interest in being one of the two reviewers for this project. Link to Pre-Registration: [LINK TO PREREG WITH COMMENT PERMISSIONS] As a reminder, the goal timeline for each review is as follows: ● Within 2 days: Reviewers are identified and signed onto the cover page of the pre-registration ready to review. ● Within 5 days: Reviewers begin to comment on the pre-registration, complete their review, and notify the Editor when they are complete. ● Within 7 days: All comments have been addressed by RR lab and Reviewers. Editor notifies COS coordination team with a final determination as to whether the protocol described is a sufficiently high fidelity, good faith project to continue. If you are interested in being one of the two reviewers for this pre-registration, and you are available to complete your review within the above timeline, please reply to this email indicating so within 24 hours. I will choose two reviewers and confirm with them that they will move forward on this project. If you do not hear back from me, please assume I have chosen other reviewers. There will be more review opportunities in the near future for you! Thank you so much for your interest in reviewing this project. Cheers, [YOUR NAME] ----- Again, we want to empower Editors to own this process to maximize recruitment efficiency, diversity, and synergy. Please let us know how we can give you the tools you need to succeed. To maximize vision and coverage, we will be maintaining a tracking spreadsheet of all prereg reviews. Please copy the SCORE Team at scorecoordinator@cos.io on any review

correspondence and keep us in loop on the status of your reviews so we can maintain our tracking system and facilitate payments. 3. Review window starts Once two Reviewers have been approved by the Editor, the review window starts. The Editor and Reviewers should have added their names to the front page of the pre-registration. The instructions for Reviewers follows: 3.1 Instructions for Reviewers: ● Review preregistered research designs for clarity, completeness, and quality. Please complete the “Final Review Checklist” on the last page of the preregistration. ● A protocol is ‘high quality’ if it provides a good faith attempt to interrogate the original finding (depending on the project type, either replicate, reproduce, or generalize). ● For replications, if the original paper provides explicit conditions that must be met to obtain the finding, then the replication should meet those conditions or have a strong justification for an alternative solution. For more information, please review this article: “What is replication?” ● For generalizability studies, please closely review the proposed methodological and analytical approach, as this may be different from the original study’s approach. ● Do not spend time reviewing the quality of the original research, plausibility of the findings, speculative possibilities beyond the original claims, or making suggestions about other things to study. ● View-only links to the original paper, as well as links to any study materials provided by the RR Lab, are provided on the pre-registration “review information” page. Whenever possible, please review any materials provided on the OSF project (e.g., study materials, power analysis documentation, analysis scripts, etc.) in addition to reviewing the preregistration itself. ● Enter comments or suggestions directly into the Google Doc of the preregistered protocol. Comments on study materials can be made as comments in the Google Doc wherever appropriate. ● Replication teams are encouraged to respond to comments by reviewers and original authors during the review period. Please work collaboratively to resolve the concerns as effectively as possible. ● Be polite, pragmatic, and thoughtful. ● When raising a concern, whenever possible, provide a potential solution. ● At the close of the review period, the Editor will review the remaining issues and resolve next steps with the Replication Team. Important files and templates for pre-registration review: Review Criteria for New Data Preregistrations (direct replications and generalizability projects) Review Criteria for Existing Data Preregistrations (data analytic replications and reproductions)

Some common issues you may encounter: 1) Check to make sure that the identified test(s) from the original study are actually being tested in the project attempt you are reviewing. Sometimes RR labs may drift from the specific focal test(s) by accident. 2) For situations like 3-way interactions, make sure there is sufficient detail to understand and accurately test the complex relationship being described. For example, if there is a gender x handedness x race interaction in the original study (left-handed white men perform worse on X), the proposal should also indicate this relationship in this detail. Stopping at saying “a significant 3-way interaction” is insufficient, as the proposed project could find a different kind of interaction (right-handed white women perform worse on x) which would be masked otherwise. 3) For existing data projects: a) Check to see if the RR lab has identified licensing information for the dataset, so we can better understand future sharing b) These projects should include the analytic code they plan to use to carry out the confirmatory analysis. If this is not the case, please ask for it. c) Please attempt to review the analytic code for the specific focal test, to ensure that the code when executed will produce the focal test output. Editors, please oversee the review process as Reviewers add comments, and please add your own as you see fit. If you feel that a comment has lingered for a few days without a response by the RR lab, please ping/remind them to respond in the document -- you may encourage them to respond via email or by assigning the comment to them as an action item. It is also your responsibility to resolve disputes that may arise in the comments. If Reviewers and the RR lab meet an impasse, it is your responsibility to find an adequate solution. 4. Review window closes We are aiming to complete pre-registration reviews within 7 calendar days. It will be the role of the Editor to oversee the timely completion of the review. We understand that some reviews may go longer than 7 days due to extensive revisions required, or potential scheduling conflicts, but keeping the COS coordination team in the loop and trying to anticipate these delays as early as possible will be great for planning future steps in the process. Once the Reviewers have completed their review, they should date their line on the “review information” page of the pre-registration. Once both Reviewers have signed off, the Editor should do a final review of the pre-registration to ensure comments made have been addressed by the RR lab. If unsatisfied, the Editor should reach out to the RR lab to request any additional, last minute revisions. Once satisfied with the pre-registration review, the Editor can sign off by dating their line on the “review information” form. When complete, the Editor will send an email (such as the example email below) to notify the SCORE team (scorecoordinator@cos.io) that the review is complete. Again, the email doesn’t

need to follow this exact format, but it should include the paper title and year in the subject, and the link to the prereg in the body. Subject: Preregistration review complete - Original Paper Title (year) Dear SCORE coordinators, I have signed off on the following review, marking it as complete. I believe the pre-registration represents a high quality, good faith attempt at the proposed project. This project can now be registered on its OSF project. Link to Pre-Registration: [LINK TO PREREG WITH COMMENT PERMISSIONS] Cheers, [YOUR NAME] 5. Checking in the inferential criteria The final step for the Editor is to “check in” the inferential criteria from the preregistration. This step serves to both confirm that the preregistration review is complete and code the inferential criteria into their component parts so that they can be incorporated into the SCORE dataset. Although it is the Editor’s responsibility to complete the check in form, Reviewers should also be aware of the target output of the preregistration so that they can help shape it during the review. The check in is accomplished by completing a google form that will be sent to the Editor for each review with a custom link. You’ll be asked to provide some information to help us identify the project for which you have just completed review, identify which claims are being evaluated by the RR attempt, and then break down the inferential criteria for each claim evaluation. Brief descriptions of each field are provided in the form for reference, but please review the following for a full description of what is expected in certain fields with examples. Additionally, the form is designed to accommodate as many claims as there might be in the RR attempt so that you should only need to submit it once per review (vs one form response per claim). The way this is accomplished is by providing well-structured responses to some items in the form that include a single line of text for each claim, which we expand into individual rows in a table on export. This is straightforward for RR attempts where only one claim is being evaluated because you will only have one line per field in the form, but it can become more challenging as more claims are added for evaluation. In the case of more than a single claim
